medical_knowledge_base.title: Dehydration After Exercise
medical_knowledge_base.guidance: Dehydration after exercise is common when fluid intake is insufficient. Drink water gradually (not all at once) with electrolytes if exercising longer than 60 minutes. Typical signs include thirst, dark urine, dizziness, and fatigue. Rehydrate within 30 minutes of exercise completion.
medical_knowledge_base.prevention: Drink 16-20 oz of water 2-3 hours before exercise, 8-10 oz 20 minutes before, and 7-10 oz every 10-20 minutes during exercise. Drink 16-24 oz after exercise for every pound lost.
medical_knowledge_base.when_to_see_doctor: Severe persistent dizziness, confusion, rapid heartbeat, or lack of sweating despite heat exposure

medical_knowledge_base.title: Exercise Overexertion
medical_knowledge_base.guidance: Overexertion occurs when training volume or intensity increases too rapidly without adequate recovery. Rest days are essential for muscle repair and adaptation. Follow the 10% rule: increase training volume by no more than 10% per week.
medical_knowledge_base.prevention: Have 1-2 complete rest days weekly. Include active recovery (light walking, stretching). Gradually increase workout intensity. Listen to your body.
medical_knowledge_base.when_to_see_doctor: Persistent severe fatigue lasting weeks, unexplained muscle pain with weakness, or signs of injury

medical_knowledge_base.title: Low Blood Sugar During/After Exercise
medical_knowledge_base.guidance: Low blood sugar (hypoglycemia) during exercise can cause dizziness and weakness. Eat a small carbohydrate-rich snack 30-60 minutes before exercise. For exercise longer than 60 minutes, consume 30-60g carbs per hour during activity. Include protein and fat to sustain energy.
medical_knowledge_base.prevention: Eat balanced meals with carbs, protein, and healthy fats. Never exercise on completely empty stomach. Have quick carbs available (banana, energy bar, sports drink).
medical_knowledge_base.when_to_see_doctor: Frequent hypoglycemic episodes, inability to recover with carbs, or suspected diabetes symptoms

medical_knowledge_base.title: Heart Palpitations and Irregular Heartbeat
medical_knowledge_base.guidance: Occasional heart palpitations can result from caffeine, dehydration, or anxiety. Mild palpitations during or after intense exercise are often normal as heart works harder. However, persistent palpitations warrant evaluation.
medical_knowledge_base.prevention: Reduce caffeine intake. Stay hydrated. Manage stress through meditation and breathing exercises. Warm up before intense exercise.
medical_knowledge_base.when_to_see_doctor: Frequent palpitations, palpitations with chest pain or shortness of breath, fainting, or persistent irregular heartbeat. This is a critical symptom requiring immediate medical attention.

medical_knowledge_base.title: Sleep Issues and Recovery
medical_knowledge_base.guidance: Quality sleep is essential for fitness recovery and immune function. Aim for 7-9 hours nightly. Avoid intense exercise 2-3 hours before bedtime as it raises body temperature and heart rate. Create a cool, dark sleeping environment.
medical_knowledge_base.prevention: Maintain consistent sleep schedule. Exercise earlier in day. Avoid caffeine after 2 PM. Limit screens 30 minutes before bed. Practice relaxation techniques.
medical_knowledge_base.when_to_see_doctor: Chronic insomnia lasting weeks, sleep apnea symptoms (loud snoring, gasping during sleep), or extreme daytime sleepiness

medical_knowledge_base.title: Delayed Onset Muscle Soreness (DOMS)
medical_knowledge_base.guidance: DOMS is normal muscular adaptation and typically peaks 24-72 hours after exercise. Soreness does not indicate injury unless accompanied by sharp pain or significant swelling. Soreness improves as body adapts.
medical_knowledge_base.prevention: Gradually increase exercise intensity. Warm up properly. Cool down and stretch. Stay hydrated. Adequate protein aids recovery.
medical_knowledge_base.when_to_see_doctor: Severe swelling, inability to move affected muscle, sharp stabbing pain, or soreness lasting beyond 5-7 days

medical_knowledge_base.title: Dizziness: Causes and When to Worry
medical_knowledge_base.guidance: Dizziness has multiple causes and context matters. Mild dizziness immediately after intense exercise often relates to dehydration or sudden position changes. Persistent dizziness or dizziness with other symptoms may indicate underlying conditions.
medical_knowledge_base.prevention: Stay hydrated. Rise slowly from lying/sitting positions. Eat regular balanced meals. Manage stress. Get adequate sleep.
medical_knowledge_base.when_to_see_doctor: Dizziness with chest pain, severe headache, difficulty speaking, weakness, numbness, vision changes, or lasting more than a few days. If dizziness comes suddenly or worsens, seek immediate medical attention.

medical_knowledge_base.title: Chest Pain During or After Exercise - CRITICAL
symptoms: pain radiating to arm or jaw
medical_knowledge_base.guidance: ANY chest pain during or after exercise requires medical evaluation. While muscle strain from exercise is often benign, chest pain can indicate serious cardiovascular issues. Do not ignore or self-diagnose.
medical_knowledge_base.prevention: Get cardiovascular fitness assessment before starting new exercise program, especially if you have risk factors. Warm up gradually.
medical_knowledge_base.when_to_see_doctor: ANY chest pain during exercise = STOP EXERCISE IMMEDIATELY AND SEEK MEDICAL ATTENTION. This is a medical emergency.

medical_knowledge_base.title: Breathing Difficulty - CRITICAL
medical_knowledge_base.guidance: While some breathlessness during intense exercise is normal, any persistent difficulty breathing warrants medical evaluation. Exercise-induced asthma is common but manageable with proper treatment.
medical_knowledge_base.prevention: Get fitness assessment before starting program. Warm up before intense exercise. Use prescribed inhalers if needed. Gradually increase intensity.
medical_knowledge_base.when_to_see_doctor: Difficulty breathing that persists after exercise stops, wheezing, repeated episodes, or breathing difficulty at rest. Seek immediate emergency care if severe.

medical_knowledge_base.title: Elevated Resting Heart Rate
symptoms: resting heart rate consistently above 100 bpm
medical_knowledge_base.guidance: Normal resting heart rate is 60-100 bpm. Trained athletes may have lower rates. Consistently elevated resting heart rate may indicate overtraining, stress, or other issues. Check resting heart rate in morning before getting up.
medical_knowledge_base.prevention: Manage stress. Reduce caffeine. Ensure adequate recovery days. Stay hydrated. Get 7-9 hours sleep.
medical_knowledge_base.when_to_see_doctor: Resting heart rate consistently above 100 bpm, accompanied by dizziness or chest pain, or sudden significant increase in resting heart rate

medical_knowledge_base.title: Nausea During or After Exercise
medical_knowledge_base.guidance: Post-exercise nausea is common and usually not serious. Avoid eating 2-3 hours before intense exercise. Eat light snacks if needed. Start with moderate intensity if new to exercise.
medical_knowledge_base.prevention: Wait 2-3 hours after large meals before intense exercise. Hydrate properly. Build intensity gradually. Exercise in cooler environments.
medical_knowledge_base.when_to_see_doctor: Nausea with vomiting and fever, persistent nausea lasting hours, nausea with chest pain or severe dizziness

medical_knowledge_base.title: Joint Pain During Exercise
medical_knowledge_base.guidance: Joint pain during exercise should not be ignored. Mild discomfort that resolves may be normal, but sharp pain or persistent issues indicate need for form correction or rest. Do not push through sharp joint pain.
medical_knowledge_base.prevention: Use proper exercise form. Warm up 5-10 minutes. Increase intensity gradually. Wear appropriate footwear. Include strength training for stability.
medical_knowledge_base.when_to_see_doctor: Sharp joint pain, significant swelling, joint that feels unstable, pain that worsens or persists for weeks

medical_knowledge_base.title: Persistent Low Energy and Fatigue
medical_knowledge_base.guidance: Fatigue lasting weeks despite adequate rest warrants investigation. Common causes include poor sleep, inadequate nutrition, or overtraining. Ensure you're eating enough calories, especially carbs for training.
medical_knowledge_base.prevention: Get 7-9 hours quality sleep. Eat balanced meals with adequate calories. Include rest days. Manage stress. Consider iron and B12 levels if concerned.
medical_knowledge_base.when_to_see_doctor: Fatigue lasting more than 2 weeks despite good sleep and nutrition, fatigue with weight loss or other symptoms, or suspected nutritional deficiency

medical_knowledge_base.title: Proper Hydration Guidelines
medical_knowledge_base.guidance: Proper hydration is essential for exercise performance and recovery. Drink when thirsty and monitor urine color (pale yellow is ideal). Needs increase with heat, humidity, and exercise intensity. Electrolytes (sodium, potassium) are important for longer workouts.
medical_knowledge_base.prevention: Drink 8-10 glasses of water daily. Before exercise: 16-20 oz 2-3 hours prior. During exercise: 7-10 oz every 10-20 minutes. After: 16-24 oz per pound lost.
medical_knowledge_base.when_to_see_doctor: Signs of severe dehydration: extreme thirst, confusion, lack of sweating despite heat, severe dizziness

medical_knowledge_base.title: Post-Exercise Nutrition and Recovery
medical_knowledge_base.guidance: Recovery nutrition within 30-60 minutes post-exercise accelerates muscle repair. Include carbohydrates to replenish glycogen and protein for muscle repair. Ratio of 3:1 or 4:1 carbs to protein is ideal.
medical_knowledge_base.prevention: Prepare post-workout snacks: banana with peanut butter, Greek yogurt with berries, chocolate milk, or protein smoothie. Include hydration with electrolytes.
medical_knowledge_base.when_to_see_doctor: Persistent poor recovery despite good nutrition and sleep warrants medical evaluation

medical_knowledge_base.title: Fever and Exercise
medical_knowledge_base.guidance: Exercising with fever can be dangerous and worsen illness. General rule: if symptoms are above neck only (runny nose, sneezing) light activity may be okay. If symptoms below neck (cough, body aches, fever) avoid exercise.
medical_knowledge_base.prevention: Get adequate sleep. Wash hands regularly. Maintain proper nutrition. Stay hydrated.
medical_knowledge_base.when_to_see_doctor: Fever above 103°F, fever lasting more than 3-5 days, fever with severe symptoms, or fever with confusion or difficulty breathing

medical_knowledge_base.title: Exercise Anxiety and Panic Attacks
medical_knowledge_base.guidance: Anxiety during exercise can mimic cardiac symptoms but is usually not dangerous. Recognizing anxiety symptoms helps reduce fear. Gradual exposure and relaxation techniques help. Exercise actually reduces long-term anxiety.
medical_knowledge_base.prevention: Start slowly with exercise. Practice deep breathing. Avoid caffeine before workouts. Progressive training builds confidence.
medical_knowledge_base.when_to_see_doctor: Anxiety that interferes with daily life, panic attacks unrelated to exercise, or difficulty distinguishing anxiety from real physical symptoms

medical_knowledge_base.title: Back Pain and Exercise
medical_knowledge_base.guidance: Back pain is common but often preventable with proper form, core strength, and flexibility. Strengthening core muscles supports spine. Stretching hip flexors and hamstrings reduces strain.
medical_knowledge_base.prevention: Maintain proper posture. Do core strengthening exercises 3x weekly. Include flexibility work. Lift with legs, not back. Take breaks if desk job.
medical_knowledge_base.when_to_see_doctor: Severe back pain, pain radiating to legs with numbness/tingling, back pain from injury, or pain lasting more than 2 weeks
